turns.content: I've been wrestling with a failing CI/CD pipeline because my function keeps allocating memory when I pass in strings that are already owned. I tried using String everywhere, but the build fails due to excessive allocations on every test run. Is there a better way than just returning a new String?

You're hitting the classic beginner trap of always allocating when you could be zero-copying instead. The solution is to use Cow<str> from std::borrow rather than plain String. When you return Cow<str> from your function, it preserves ownership if input was owned or borrows if it wasn't, so your CI pipeline stops spewing allocations.

That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how to use Cow<[T]> for vectors since my tests also involve mutable data. Can you show me the exact trait impls or function signatures that make this work in a real pipeline?

For vectors, you do the same thing with Cow<[T]> which implements Clone on Write for any type T. Your pipeline functions should return Cow<[u8]>, Cow<[String]>, or generic Cow<[T]> depending on your data. This lets you avoid cloning entire buffers when they're already owned, cutting down memory pressure dramatically in your test suites.
